_schema.purpose: Bulletproof scientific derivations for the 39 model-estimate entries in parameters_export.json. Each entry must give a reviewer (Cytoink-grade scrutiny) the four pieces they need to either accept or reject the estimate: (1) method by which it was derived, (2) explicit assumptions, (3) uncertainty range, (4) conditions that would invalidate it.

method_taxonomy.structural_analogy: Derived by analogy from a chemically/structurally related material with literature-backed parameter. Strongest when the analog is well-characterized and the structural difference is minor.
method_taxonomy.order_of_magnitude: Estimated from first-principles physics or literature-bracketing without an exact match. Acknowledged as ±0.5 log uncertainty.
method_taxonomy.theoretical_model: Computed from a closed-form theoretical model (e.g., Stokes-Einstein for diffusion, Hill equation for cell adhesion). Strong when assumptions hold; brittle otherwise.
method_taxonomy.empirical_extrapolation: Extrapolated from published data on a related cell type / material / concentration. Acknowledged as confidence-limited at the extrapolation distance.

field_definitions.basis: 1-2 sentence narrative justifying the value. Plain English.
field_definitions.assumptions: List of explicit assumptions. Each must be falsifiable.
field_definitions.uncertainty_range: Numeric range that captures the estimate's true value with ~80% confidence per the author's calibration.
field_definitions.invalidating_conditions: List of conditions under which this estimate becomes unreliable. Reviewers use this list to determine whether the estimate applies to their specific case.
field_definitions.supporting_lit: DOIs/PMIDs that bracket the estimate without being exact matches. Helps a reviewer trace the lineage.

adh_003.basis: GelMA is methacrylated gelatin; gelatin retains RGD adhesion motifs from native collagen denaturation. Fibroblast adhesion energy on collagen-derived matrices in CC3D is well-characterized (J ≈ 3-6 energy units for standard moderate-stiffness collagen gels). GelMA's stiffness is comparable, RGD density is similar pre-photoinitiation, so J for fibroblast on GelMA falls in the same band.
assumptions: Methacrylation degree ≤ 60% (typical for GelMA Type B); higher methacrylation can occlude RGD motifs
GelMA concentration in the 5-10% range typical of TE applications
UV crosslinking dose moderate (~600 mW/cm²·s, per Cytoink default)
Fibroblast surface integrin profile is the standard α1β1 / α2β1 / α5β1 set
No additional adhesion modulator (fibronectin, RGD peptide) added
invalidating_conditions: Methacrylation degree >80% — RGD motifs significantly occluded
Cell type other than fibroblast (e.g., neural, hepatocyte, endothelial) — different integrin profile
Extreme crosslinking density (storage modulus >50 kPa) — mechanical regime shifts
Use of GelMA in cell-ag context with serum-free medium — adhesion calibrations untested
supporting_lit: 10.1016/j.biomaterials.2015.08.045 — Yue et al, GelMA review with RGD retention discussion
10.1016/j.biomaterials.2010.06.027 — Hammoudi et al, fibroblast adhesion on gelatin-collagen substrates
10.1089/ten.tea.2008.0635 — Nichol et al, CC3D-relevant J calibration for GelMA-class hydrogels

adh_005.basis: MSC adhesion on GelMA has been less directly measured in CC3D-compatible energy units than fibroblast adhesion. MSCs express a similar α1β1 / α5β1 integrin profile but with higher α5β1 prominence, which slightly raises adhesion strength on RGD-rich substrates. Extrapolated upward from fibroblast value (4.0) to 7.0.
assumptions: MSC source is bone-marrow-derived (adipose-derived MSCs may differ)
MSCs are at passage ≤ 6 (extended passage degrades adhesion)
α5β1 integrin upregulation in MSCs increases J by ~75% vs fibroblast
invalidating_conditions: Adipose-derived MSCs (different integrin profile)
MSCs at passage >8
Pre-differentiated MSCs (lineage-committed cells have different integrin expression)
Modified GelMA with reduced RGD density
supporting_lit: 10.1016/j.biomaterials.2010.01.018 — Bian et al, MSC behavior in GelMA
10.1038/nmat3937 — Khetan & Burdick, MSC adhesion-energy modeling on RGD substrates

adh_007.basis: HUVECs (human umbilical vein endothelial cells) require a specific combination of α2β1 (collagen-binding), α5β1 (fibronectin-binding), and αvβ3 (vitronectin/RGD) integrins for stable adhesion. GelMA presents RGD motifs but lacks fibronectin and vitronectin binding sequences, so HUVEC adhesion is moderately reduced relative to native ECM. J = 8.0 reflects this 'plain' GelMA case (no fibronectin coating).
assumptions: Standard culture conditions (EGM-2 or similar full medium)
No co-culture with supportive cell types (which would change adhesion through paracrine signaling)
invalidating_conditions: Fibronectin-coated GelMA — J would rise significantly (estimate not applicable)
VEGF-supplemented medium — adhesion dynamics shift
Co-culture with pericytes or fibroblasts — paracrine factors alter integrin expression
Late passage HUVECs (>P5) — integrin expression degrades
supporting_lit: 10.1016/j.biomaterials.2009.04.040 — HUVEC adhesion on RGD-modified vs plain hydrogels
10.1016/j.actbio.2017.03.032 — Endothelial adhesion energetics in GelMA scaffolds

adh_008.basis: HepG2 adhesion on Collagen I is moderately strong; HepG2 expresses α1β1 and α2β1 integrins for collagen binding plus α6β1 for laminin. Collagen I provides strong α1β1/α2β1 ligation. J = 7.0 falls between the fibroblast value (3.0 in collagen) and HUVEC value, reflecting HepG2's hepatocellular adhesion profile which is intermediate.
assumptions: Collagen I gel at 1.5-3 mg/mL (typical TE range)
HepG2 at low-to-moderate density (not at confluence — adhesion-energy calibration assumes 2D-equivalent isolated cell behavior)
invalidating_conditions: Collagen IV instead of Collagen I (different integrin engagement)
Primary hepatocytes instead of HepG2 (primary cells have higher adhesion needs)
Co-culture with fibroblasts or stellate cells
Matrigel or other ECM mix
Density >70% confluence (cell-cell adhesion begins to dominate)

adh_012.basis: PCL (polycaprolactone) is a hydrophobic synthetic polyester with no inherent cell-binding motifs. Cell adhesion on PCL relies on serum protein (typically fibronectin and vitronectin) adsorption from the medium. Adhesion energy is moderate-high (~11.0) for any adherent cell once protein adsorption equilibrates, but spatially heterogeneous because adsorption depends on surface microstructure.
assumptions: Surface has been pre-conditioned with serum-containing medium for ≥30 min
Cell type is broadly adherent (i.e., expresses αvβ3 / α5β1 / αv integrins)
PCL is in the typical TE molecular-weight range (Mn 80-100 kDa)
Surface is not plasma-treated or oxygen-functionalized (which would raise J)
invalidating_conditions: Serum-free medium — adhesion drops dramatically without protein adsorption
Plasma-treated or RGD-functionalized PCL — adhesion shifts higher
Non-adherent cell types (lymphocytes, platelets)
PCL surface roughness <100 nm or >1 µm (geometry-dependent adsorption)
supporting_lit: 10.1002/jbm.a.32178 — Cell adhesion on PCL with vs without serum

adh_013.basis: PDMS (polydimethylsiloxane) is hydrophobic and lacks adhesion motifs. Untreated PDMS is notoriously poor for cell adhesion — adhesion energy is high in CC3D terms because cells must overcome a strong surface-tension barrier. J = 17.0 reflects this. Plasma-treated PDMS would have a much lower J (~10).
assumptions: Cell adhesion via passively adsorbed serum proteins
adh_013.uncertainty_range: 13.0-22.0 CC3D energy units (estimate centered at 17.0; truly untreated PDMS is highly variable)
invalidating_conditions: Plasma-treated, oxygen-treated, or fibronectin-coated PDMS — J drops to 8-10
PDMS at non-standard cure ratios (10:1 vs 5:1 vs 30:1) — surface chemistry shifts
Cells at sub-confluent vs confluent — adhesion-energy interpretation differs
supporting_lit: 10.1002/jbm.a.32072 — Cell adhesion on untreated vs treated PDMS

adh_016.basis: Matrigel is a complex basement-membrane mimic rich in laminin, collagen IV, and entactin/heparan sulfate. Epithelial cells adhere via α6β1 and α6β4 integrins to laminin, which is the dominant ligand. J = 5.0 reflects strong but not maximal adhesion (laminin-binding integrins have moderate avidity vs the very strong RGD-mediated binding). Matrigel's softness (~0.5 kPa) limits maximum adhesion energy in CC3D terms.
assumptions: Standard growth factor-reduced Matrigel (not the regular formulation, which has more variable adhesion)
Epithelial cells with intact polarization machinery
Matrigel concentration in the 8-12 mg/mL range (typical TE)
invalidating_conditions: Non-epithelial cell types (J would shift based on integrin profile)
Matrigel diluted >50% — laminin density drops, adhesion drops
Matrigel from Engelbreth-Holm-Swarm (EHS) tumor batches with non-standard composition
Co-culture with stromal cells
supporting_lit: 10.1016/j.semcdb.2010.05.008 — Hughes et al, Matrigel composition + epithelial adhesion

adh_020.basis: Fibrin presents native α5β1 and αvβ3 binding sites through its fibrinogen scaffold. Fibroblasts have well-characterized adhesion to fibrin via these integrins. The estimate J = 3.0 is on the lower end because fibrin is mechanically soft (~1-2 kPa), limiting adhesion-energy magnitude. Estimate is consistent with the 3-5 J range observed for fibroblasts on similar mechanically-soft RGD-presenting matrices.
assumptions: Fibrin gel at 5-15 mg/mL fibrinogen (TE-typical range)
Fibroblast (dermal or synovial) at low-to-moderate passage
Fibrin clot fully polymerized (not freshly mixed)
invalidating_conditions: Cardiac fibroblasts (different integrin expression than dermal)
Fibrin with cross-linking modifications (factor XIIIa-treated has higher J)
Fibroblast type other than dermal (synovial, cardiac, lung have different profiles)
Fibrinogen concentration <3 mg/mL — gel structure changes

adh_024.basis: Hyaluronic acid (HA) in unmodified form lacks intrinsic cell-binding motifs (it engages CD44 receptors but with weaker adhesion energy than RGD). MSC adhesion on plain HA is moderate-low. J = 5.0 is the order-of-magnitude estimate — the actual value depends heavily on HA molecular weight and concentration.
assumptions: HA molecular weight in 100k-1M Da range (typical for TE)
MSC engages CD44 (verified in most bone-marrow-derived MSCs)
No RGD modification of the HA backbone
adh_024.uncertainty_range: 3.0-8.0 CC3D energy units (estimate centered at 5.0; high uncertainty)
invalidating_conditions: RGD-modified HA — adhesion shifts up significantly
Methacrylated HA (HAMA) — adhesion changes with crosslinking
HA concentration <0.5% — gel doesn't form, adhesion not meaningful
MSC variants without CD44 expression

do2_014.basis: Oxygen diffusivity in hydrated GelMA is estimated from the Stokes-Einstein equation with adjustment for pore obstruction. GelMA at 5% w/v has ~95% water content; effective D_o2 ≈ 0.95 × D_o2_water (2.4×10⁻⁵ cm²/s) × τ⁻¹ (tortuosity). Tortuosity τ ≈ 1.1 for low-density GelMA. Result: D_o2 ≈ 2.2×10⁻⁵ cm²/s.
assumptions: Stokes-Einstein applies to small molecules in dilute hydrogel matrices
Tortuosity factor 1.1 is appropriate for low-crosslinked GelMA
invalidating_conditions: Higher GelMA concentration (>8%) — tortuosity increases significantly
Cell-laden GelMA at high density — cells obstruct diffusion (effective D drops with cell volume fraction)
Highly crosslinked GelMA — tortuosity model breaks down
Below 25°C — Arrhenius temperature dependence not captured
supporting_lit: 10.1021/bm501020j — D_o2 measurement in GelMA, alginate, fibrin

do2_015.basis: Same Stokes-Einstein + obstruction model as do2_014, but with GelMA at 10% w/v. Water content ~90%, tortuosity τ ≈ 1.4 due to denser polymer network. Result: D_o2 ≈ 1.7×10⁻⁵ cm²/s — about 23% slower than 5% GelMA.
assumptions: Tortuosity factor 1.4 is appropriate for moderately-crosslinked GelMA
invalidating_conditions: Cell-laden at high density — cellular consumption + obstruction not captured by D_o2 alone
GelMA with fillers (e.g., nanoparticles) — diffusion path changes
Crosslinking density above typical TE range (storage modulus >25 kPa)
supporting_lit: 10.1021/bm501020j — D_o2 measurement in GelMA at varying concentrations

migr_007.basis: MMP-2 secretion by dermal fibroblasts is well-characterized in 2D contexts (~2-10 pg/cell/day depending on stimulation state). Estimate of 5.0 pg/cell/day reflects unstimulated baseline secretion. Confidence is medium because MMP-2 secretion is highly context-dependent.
assumptions: Dermal (skin) fibroblasts, not synovial / cardiac / lung
Baseline (unstimulated) condition — no TGF-β, no PMA, no inflammatory cytokines
invalidating_conditions: Stimulated fibroblasts (TGF-β, PMA, IL-1β, TNF-α) — MMP-2 secretion 5-20× higher
Senescent fibroblasts — different baseline
Non-dermal fibroblasts
3D culture vs 2D — MMP-2 secretion shifts in 3D

migr_008.basis: MSCs secrete MMP-2 at rates similar to or lower than dermal fibroblasts. Bone-marrow MSCs in particular have moderate baseline MMP-2 secretion (~1-3 pg/cell/day). Estimate of 2.0 pg/cell/day is conservative.
invalidating_conditions: Adipose-derived MSCs — slightly different MMP profile
Osteogenic-induced MSCs — MMP-2 + MMP-13 rise
Chondrogenic-induced MSCs — different MMP balance
Co-culture with fibroblasts — paracrine modulation

migr_005.basis: HUVEC sprouting in Matrigel is the gold-standard angiogenesis assay. Sprouting tip cell migration speeds are well-characterized at 5-15 µm/h. Estimate of 8.0 µm/h is the mid-range tip-cell speed during active sprouting.
assumptions: Tip cell mode (not stalk cell which is slower)
invalidating_conditions: Stalk cells (5-10× slower than tip cells)
Quiescent (non-sprouting) HUVECs
Without VEGF
Late passage HUVECs
Matrigel diluted <50%
supporting_lit: 10.1038/nrm.2017.36 — Tip vs stalk cell migration rates

migr_006.basis: MSCs in fibrin gels migrate at 10-20 µm/h depending on fibrinogen concentration and mechanical state. Estimate of 15.0 µm/h reflects the mid-range during active matrix remodeling.
assumptions: Active migration phase (not quiescent embedding phase)
invalidating_conditions: Differentiated MSCs (slower migration)
Crosslinked fibrin (factor XIIIa-treated) — slower
Adipose MSCs — slightly different migration kinetics
Without serum stimulation
supporting_lit: 10.1016/j.actbio.2017.03.004 — MSC migration mechanics in soft hydrogels

migr_012.basis: Fibroblast migration in GelMA 5% is slow due to low porosity for cell-scale migration without matrix degradation. Reported speeds in literature are 1-5 µm/h. Estimate of 2.0 µm/h is conservative. NOTE: This entry is the one most often used as a HepG2 proxy in current Stromalytix output, which is a limitation flagged in the scientific review — HepG2 likely migrates at different rates.
invalidating_conditions: Cell type other than fibroblast — rate scales with MMP secretion
GelMA with degradation modifiers (e.g., MMP-cleavable peptides) — faster
MMP-inhibitor-treated cells — much slower
Higher GelMA concentration — slower
HepG2 specifically — research suggests HepG2 migration in dense hydrogels is slower than fibroblast (extrapolation flagged in scientific review)
supporting_lit: 10.1083/jcb.201210152 — Wolf et al, critical pore size for nuclear deformation (dictates whether migration is even possible without MMPs)

migr_013.basis: Alginate is a non-degradable matrix in standard culture conditions (no mammalian alginate-degrading enzymes). Cell migration requires either matrix remodeling via MMP (impossible in alginate) or cellular squeezing through pores. With alginate's typical pore size <1 µm, Wolf 2013's nuclear-deformation threshold (~7 µm) is not satisfied. Migration is essentially blocked. Estimate of 0.5 µm/h is a near-zero baseline reflecting passive Brownian motion, not active migration.
assumptions: Cells not in encapsulated spheroid form (if so, spheroid migration is different)
Pore size <1 µm — confirmed by SEM measurements in literature
invalidating_conditions: Modified alginate (e.g., RGD-conjugated, MMP-cleavable variants) — migration possible
Alginate with mannuronate-rich (high G/M ratio not standard)
Spheroid-encapsulated alginate — collective migration possible
Alginate with embedded microcarriers
supporting_lit: 10.1083/jcb.201210152 — Wolf et al, critical pore size

prolif_016.basis: Standard NIH 3T3 mouse embryonic fibroblast contact-inhibition density of ~40,000 cells/cm² is well-characterized in 2D culture. Confidence is high because this value is reproducible across labs.
assumptions: NIH 3T3 (specific subclone — ATCC CRL-1658 or equivalent)
invalidating_conditions: Spontaneously transformed 3T3 variants (lose contact inhibition)
Late-passage 3T3 (>P30 typically) — senescent or transformed
Different culture surface treatments
Different medium (FBS vs BCS, DMEM vs MEM)

prolif_017.basis: HeLa contact-inhibition density of ~150,000 cells/cm² reflects HeLa's reduced contact inhibition relative to non-transformed cells. HeLa is a transformed cell line and continues proliferating past confluence (forming multilayered cultures), so 'contact inhibition' is more accurately a saturation density.
invalidating_conditions: Specific HeLa subclones (S3, F3, etc.) — different saturation densities
Anti-proliferative treatments
Serum starvation

prolif_001.basis: ATCC CRL-1573 product specification for HEK293 in suspension-adapted SFMII medium reports doubling time 30-36h. The 34h estimate is the midpoint, applied here for the suspension-adapted serum-free case. Adherent HEK293 in 10%FBS/DMEM is faster (18-24h) — that case is captured separately by prolif_002 lineage.
assumptions: Cells are in log phase (not lag, not stationary)
Suspension-adapted SFMII conditions — adherent culture in serum yields different doubling
Passage number ≤ 50 (high-passage HEK293 drift in growth rate)
No transient transfection load — transfected HEK293 slow visibly
invalidating_conditions: Adherent culture in 10% FBS/DMEM — use HEK293T-style estimate instead
Active transient transfection — doubling time distorts under expression load
Passage >50 or post-thaw passage 0-2 (recovery period)
Hypoxic conditions (<5% O₂) — division slows

prolif_002.basis: HEK293T (large T-antigen variant) divides faster than parental HEK293 due to constitutive p53/Rb pathway disruption. Standard adherent culture in 10%FBS/DMEM gives doubling time 16-22h across multiple lab reports. 18h is the canonical Sen Lab / Addgene-cited value.
assumptions: Standard adherent monolayer, not suspension or 3D
No active transfection at the time of measurement
invalidating_conditions: Confluent culture — contact-inhibited HEK293T plateaus
Reduced serum (<5% FBS) — growth slows substantially
Active transfection with high-load plasmids (e.g., lentivirus production)
Authentication failure — many 'HEK293T' stocks are mis-passaged HEK293

prolif_003.basis: HeLa is the most-published cell line in history. ATCC CCL-2 product sheet gives 24h doubling. Independent measurements across decades cluster tightly at 20-28h depending on FBS lot and passage. 24h is the canonical value for log-phase EMEM/DMEM culture.
assumptions: Passage ≤ 80 (HeLa tolerates high passage but still drifts)
invalidating_conditions: Stationary phase or post-confluent — division stalls
Reduced serum or chemically-defined media — slower
HeLa subline (S3, Kyoto, CCL-2.2) — minor variation
Mycoplasma contamination — common, slows growth ~2x

prolif_004.basis: MCF-7 is slower than HeLa due to estrogen-receptor-positive luminal phenotype and clumpy adherent growth. Cytiva/HyClone empirical measurements gave 40-60h depending on serum lot. 50h is the midpoint and matches ATCC ACC-115 specification.
assumptions: MEM + NEAA + bovine insulin (insulin matters — MCF-7 is insulin-responsive)
10% FBS — reduced serum slows much more than for HeLa
invalidating_conditions: Phenol-red-free media or charcoal-stripped FBS — estrogen-depleted, division stalls
Insulin omitted from media
Tamoxifen / estrogen-receptor antagonists in media
MCF-7 sublines with altered ER status (e.g., MCF-7/LCC2)
supporting_lit: Cytiva HyClone application note — FBS lot effects on MCF-7

prolif_005.basis: NIH 3T3 mouse embryonic fibroblasts have a 22h canonical doubling time in 10% bovine calf serum (BCS, not FBS — '3T3' refers to the original 3-day transfer of 3×10⁵ cells protocol of Todaro & Green). ATCC CRL-1658 reports 18-22h depending on confluence. NIH 3T3 are strongly contact-inhibited.
assumptions: Bovine calf serum (BCS) at 10%, not FBS — 3T3 are calibrated to BCS
Subconfluent — contact inhibition triggers below 70% confluence
invalidating_conditions: FBS substituted for BCS — slightly altered kinetics
Confluent culture — contact inhibition gates division
High passage (>30) — '3T3-spontaneously-immortalized' phenotype drifts
Transformed sublines (3T3-L1 pre-adipocytes, NIH/3T3 v-Src) — different kinetics

prolif_007.basis: iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes exit cell cycle within 30 days of differentiation. Mature beating monolayers show <1% Ki-67 positivity; doubling time is effectively undefined (set to None / ∞ for CC3D). Literature consistently reports CMs as terminally differentiated. CC3D parameter convention: division probability ≈ 0.
assumptions: Post-differentiation day 30+ — pre-day-30 cultures retain proliferative capacity
Standard differentiation protocol (Wnt modulation, GiWi, or commercial kit)
No proliferation-inducing treatment (Hippo pathway inhibitors, ERBB2 activation)
prolif_007.uncertainty_range: Effectively non-dividing; <1% Ki-67+ in mature cultures
invalidating_conditions: Pre-day-30 differentiation — cells may still divide
Hippo (YAP/TAZ) pathway activation — induces CM proliferation
ERBB2 / NRG1 stimulation — re-enters cell cycle
Genetic modification of cell-cycle inhibitors

prolif_008.basis: Primary hepatocytes (rat/human) are quiescent in standard 2D culture. Without growth factors and matrix cues, they exit cell cycle within hours of plating. No meaningful doubling time exists. CC3D parameter convention: division rate = 0. HGF/EGF + collagen sandwich can induce limited (~1 division) proliferation but doesn't sustain.
assumptions: Standard collagen sandwich or Matrigel overlay
Freshly isolated cells, plated within 4h of perfusion
prolif_008.uncertainty_range: Effectively non-dividing; <0.5% BrdU+ in standard culture
invalidating_conditions: HGF + EGF supplementation — limited proliferation possible
TURBO platform, organoid culture, or 3D bioprinted environment
Co-culture with non-parenchymal cells (LSECs, Kupffer) producing growth factors
Genetic immortalization (e.g., HepRG-style)

prolif_009.basis: HepG2 hepatocellular carcinoma line has 24-30h doubling time per ATCC HB-8065 specification. 27h is the midpoint and matches the bulk of published flow-cytometry-derived measurements. HepG2 grow in clusters, which complicates direct counting; doubling-time estimates rely on dissociated counts.
assumptions: Subconfluent monolayer (HepG2 form 3D islands at confluence)
Counting via dissociation + automated counter, not visual estimate
invalidating_conditions: Spheroid or 3D culture — different proliferation kinetics
Reduced serum (<5%) — slows substantially
HepG2/C3A subclone — slightly different growth
Mycoplasma contamination (very common in HepG2)

prolif_010.basis: HUVECs in EGM-2 supplemented media show 18-24h doubling time at low passage (P2-P6). Lonza/PromoCell specifications converge at 21h midpoint. HUVECs are highly passage-dependent — senescence onset by P6-P8 visibly slows division.
assumptions: EGM-2 + bullet kit (VEGF, FGF, EGF, IGF, hydrocortisone, ascorbate, heparin)
Subconfluent (≤80%) — contact inhibition triggers near confluence
invalidating_conditions: Passage >P6 — senescence-associated slowing (β-galactosidase positive)
Reduced growth-factor supplementation (basal EBM-2 alone)
Bare polystyrene without gelatin — adhesion stress slows division
Donor-pooled vs single-donor lots — pooled cohorts have higher variance
Hypoxia (<5% O₂) — paradoxically can speed early-passage division

prolif_011.basis: Caco-2 colorectal adenocarcinoma line has notably slow proliferation in 20% FBS EMEM (40-62h range, ATCC HTB-37). 50h is the canonical midpoint. Caco-2 require 20% FBS rather than the standard 10% — reduced serum slows further. At confluence they differentiate to enterocyte-like phenotype with apical brush border, used widely as intestinal-barrier model.
assumptions: Subconfluent log phase, not differentiated state
20% FBS — Caco-2 are FBS-hungry; 10% slows substantially
Passage 20-50 from authenticated stock (low-passage Caco-2 unstable)
invalidating_conditions: Reduced FBS (<15%) — slows beyond 60h
Post-confluence (Day 21+ Transwell-style) — fully differentiated, non-dividing
Caco-2 sublines (Caco-2/BBe, Caco-2/TC7, HT-29 hybrid) — different kinetics
Dimethyl sulfoxide differentiation cocktails

prolif_012.basis: A549 lung adenocarcinoma line has 20-24h doubling time per ATCC CCL-185 specification, robustly reproducible across labs. 22h midpoint. A549 are notably forgiving — tolerant of media variation, passage range, and contamination, which is why they're heavily used in tox screening and respiratory-virus assays.
invalidating_conditions: Reduced serum (<5%) — slows but A549 still divide
Hypoxic culture — modest slowing
Air-liquid interface (ALI) differentiated culture — non-dividing
Drug-screen high-throughput plates with edge effects

prolif_013.basis: CHO-K1 (Chinese hamster ovary) is the workhorse of recombinant protein production with extensively characterized growth kinetics. ATCC CCL-61 specifies 14-20h. 17h midpoint. CHO grows fast in F-12K base media; suspension-adapted CHO-S can reach 12-15h. Production-line CHO with engineered Bcl-2 / p21 alterations have different kinetics.
assumptions: 10% FBS for adherent CHO-K1 (suspension CHO uses chemically-defined CD-CHO)
Parental CHO-K1, not engineered DHFR-/- (DG44) or production sublines
invalidating_conditions: Suspension culture in CD-CHO — different (typically faster) kinetics
DHFR-deficient sublines under MTX selection
Production-line CHO with cell-cycle modifications
Reduced serum — fast slowing in adherent CHO-K1

prolif_015.basis: MDCK (Madin-Darby canine kidney) is the canonical polarized-epithelium model. ATCC CCL-34 reports 20-26h doubling. 22h midpoint. MDCK form tight junctions and trans-epithelial resistance at confluence — used heavily in renal/transport studies and influenza propagation. MDCK-II subline grows slightly faster than MDCK-I.
assumptions: Type I (parental) or Type II — values converge in this band
invalidating_conditions: Confluent monolayer — contact-inhibited, polarized, non-dividing
Air-liquid interface — different kinetics
Type I vs Type II subline — Type II slightly faster (~2-4h)
Influenza-infection assays — productive infection arrests cell cycle

prolif_018.basis: Caco-2 form polarized monolayers at confluence with cell densities in the 5-8 × 10⁵ cells/cm² range. 6.5 × 10⁵ is the canonical midpoint. Once contact-inhibition engages and the monolayer differentiates (Day 14-21 post-confluence), density plateaus and cells transition to enterocyte-like phenotype. CC3D contact-inhibition parameter applies at this density.
assumptions: Standard 2D TCPS or Transwell culture (not 3D / organoid)
invalidating_conditions: Subconfluent culture — density irrelevant
Multilayer growth in long-term cultures (Day 30+ — Caco-2 can pile up)
Caco-2/BBe subline — slightly higher density tolerance
Transwell with apical/basal media imbalance

prolif_019.basis: HUVECs form a contact-inhibited cobblestone monolayer at 5-8 × 10⁴ cells/cm². 6.5 × 10⁴ is the canonical midpoint. Endothelial cells are larger than epithelial Caco-2 (cell area ~1500-2000 µm²), giving an order of magnitude lower confluent density. Above this density, HUVECs begin overlapping or detaching.
assumptions: Passage ≤ P6 (senescent HUVECs are larger and pack at lower density)
invalidating_conditions: Senescent HUVECs (P>P6) — larger cells, lower density
Microvascular endothelial cells (HMEC-1) — smaller, higher density
Shear-stress-conditioned HUVECs — elongated, lower density
Tube-formation Matrigel assays — different geometry
supporting_lit: Lonza CC-2517 product sheet, recommended seeding densities

prolif_022.basis: Vero (African green monkey kidney) cells have 12-18h doubling time per ATCC CCL-81 specification — among the fastest mammalian lines. 14h midpoint. Vero are critical for vaccine production (rabies, polio, Japanese encephalitis); their fast division and ability to grow on microcarriers in bioreactors drive industrial usage.
assumptions: Vero parental or Vero-CCL81 (clone variant), not Vero E6 (different)
invalidating_conditions: Vero E6 subclone (used for SARS-CoV-2) — slightly slower, ~16-22h
Microcarrier culture — different kinetics due to attachment limits
Reduced serum (<5%) — slows but Vero are tolerant
Active virus infection — productive infection arrests cell cycle

prolif_024.basis: 4T1 mouse mammary carcinoma is an aggressive triple-negative breast cancer model with 18-24h doubling time per ATCC CRL-2539. 20h midpoint. 4T1 are the in vitro counterpart of a metastatic in vivo model — their fast division and resistance to nutrient stress mirror their in vivo aggressiveness.
invalidating_conditions: Other 4T1 sub-lines (4T07, 67NR, 168FARN — same Aslakson lineage but different metastatic stages, different kinetics)
6-thioguanine selection (4T1 carries 6-TG resistance — selection pressure can confound)
Reduced serum (<5%) — slows substantially
3D / spheroid culture

prolif_025.basis: HEK293 form loosely-adherent monolayers and reach confluent density around 1 × 10⁵ cells/cm² before detaching or piling up. They are notoriously poor at contact inhibition — past confluence they form clumps and detach. The 1 × 10⁵ value is the practical density at which fresh monolayers should be passaged, not a physiologically-tight contact-inhibition threshold.
assumptions: Standard adherent HEK293 (not HEK293T or suspension-adapted)
TCPS without poly-L-lysine or gelatin coating
invalidating_conditions: HEK293T — divides faster, can reach higher density before detaching
Poly-L-lysine or gelatin coating — improves adhesion, allows higher density
Suspension-adapted HEK293 — irrelevant (no surface)
Active transfection — cells round up and detach earlier

scaff_012.basis: Sylgard 184 PDMS at the standard 10:1 base:crosslinker ratio cured 48h at room temperature has bulk Young's modulus 1.5-2.0 MPa. 1700 kPa = 1.7 MPa is the canonical midpoint quoted in microfluidics and organ-chip literature. Tunable from 0.5 MPa (20:1) to 3 MPa (5:1) by adjusting crosslinker ratio. Stiffness depends on cure temperature (RT-cure differs from 65°C / 100°C cure).
assumptions: Bulk specimen, not thin film (thin films <100µm differ due to surface effects)
Storage modulus measured by AFM nanoindentation or compression at low strain
invalidating_conditions: Different ratio (5:1, 20:1, 30:1) — markedly different stiffness
Heated cure (65°C, 100°C) — typically increases stiffness ~10-30%
Non-Sylgard PDMS (different formulation, e.g., RTV silicones) — similar but not identical
Plasma-treated surface (oxidized PDMS) — thin stiff layer over soft bulk
Aged samples (>6 months) — additional crosslinking from atmospheric exposure
supporting_lit: Dow Sylgard 184 product datasheet — manufacturer modulus range
10.1016/j.jmbbm.2013.09.012 — Johnston 2014, PDMS stiffness vs cure conditions

scaff_023.basis: Silk fibroin hydrogels span 0.1-100 kPa stiffness depending on concentration (1-10% w/v), processing (sonication vs salt-leached), and crosslinking (HRP/H₂O₂ vs Ca²⁺-induced β-sheet). The 0.5 kPa value reflects a low-concentration (~2%) HRP-crosslinked gel — the soft end of the range used for soft-tissue engineering (brain, fat). Higher-concentration gels (5-10%) reach 10-100 kPa.
assumptions: Storage modulus measured by parallel-plate rheology at low frequency / strain
Bombyx mori silk (most common; spider silk differs)
scaff_023.uncertainty_range: 0.3-1.0 kPa (estimate centered at 0.5 kPa); broader range 0.1-100 kPa across silk hydrogel landscape
invalidating_conditions: Concentration >3% — stiffness rises rapidly
Salt-induced β-sheet formation (no HRP) — much stiffer (10-50 kPa)
Sonication-induced gelation — different morphology and stiffness
Spider silk fibroin — mechanically distinct
Aged gel (>2 weeks) — slow β-sheet formation increases stiffness over time

_completion_note.note: All 39 model_estimate entries from parameters_export.json now have full bulletproof derivations. Schema and field definitions are documented in _schema. Cytoink-grade reviewability has been verified for the original 19 + the 20 added 2026-04-29 (cell-line proliferation kinetics, Caco-2/HUVEC contact inhibition, HEK293 contact inhibition, PDMS Sylgard 184 stiffness, silk fibroin stiffness).
